result.text: ESPN analyst says Miami can challenge Purdue in NCAA tournament 2026 Skip to main content Home Heat Dolphins Marlins High School Fishing Hurricanes Gators Seminoles Owls Golf USA TODAY Sports SportsbookWire Jay Bilas said Purdue's March Madness path was easy. What about Miami? Dan Rorabaugh USA TODAY NETWORK - Florida Show Caption Hide Caption Who’s ready to put on the glass slipper this March? Two underdog teams that could make a Cinderella run this March Madness are Saint Louis and Miami (Ohio). Bracketologists seemed to be writing off Miami after their fate was revealed in the 2026 NCAA men's basketball tournament . At least one ESPN analyst came to the Hurricanes' defense. The Canes received a No. 7 seed in the West Region and will face No. 10 Missouri in what's effectively a road game in their first-round matchup in St. Louis . If they win to continue their March Madness expedition, Miami would likely get No. 2 Purdue in the second round (unless 15-seed Queens University pulls off a massive upset). The CBS selection show didn't spend much time discussing UM and its prospects in the bracket, perhaps because their lead-in was the Boilermakers knocking off Michigan to win the Big Ten tournament championship. And it seemed ESPN's Bracketology show would go on the same route. Buy Miami NCAA tournament tickets Jay Bilas got the first shot at analyzing the West Region and while he thought top-seeded Arizona had a tough draw, he felt differently about the bottom half of the bracket. "I think Purdue has a pretty decent draw here," Bilas said. In fairness, he did go on to say "clearly Miami-Missouri, that winner's not going to be an easy second-round game" before expounding on the weaknesses of No. 3-seed Gonzaga and No. 6 BYU. But then former North Carolina guard Jay Williams stepped in to challenge the Duke alum. "I think Purdue will be OK, but Miami's a really good team," Williams said. "Miami's played well. Jai Lucas has done one heck of a job with this team, obviously Tre Donaldson is a guy who can knock down shots, you think about Shelton Henderson, a guy that can attack ..." "And (Malik) Reneau is a matchup problem," Seth Greenberg chimed in before Williams talked up Ernest Udeh Jr.'s abilities on both ends. "So yes, I do like Purdue, but that could be a good matchup against Trey Kaufman-Renn and how they like to dominate the boards," Williams concluded. Miami has had an excellent season under its first-year coach, going from 7-24 to 25-8 and reaching the ACC tournament semifinals. The Hurricanes beat North Carolina when both Caleb Wilson and Seth Trimble (big man Henri Veesaar was out with an injury). And they've got two All-ACC players in Reneau and Donaldson. Here's hoping Purdue doesn't have a repeat of 2023, when they became the second No. 1 seed to lose in the first round. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning

CHICAGO — Four games in four days, culminating with a Big Ten Tournament championship game against perhaps the best team in the country. Purdue basketball was up for the challenge. Oscar Cluff's dunk with 1:17 to go put a stamp on the Boilermakers ' 80-72 title game victory over regular-season Big Ten champion Michigan on Sunday, March 15 at the United Center. Cluff sealed the title win at the free-throw line late, along with four straight makes from Jack Benter. It's the third Big Ten Tournament championship for Purdue, which won in 2009 and 2023, the latter also at United Center. Purdue basketball turning point vs Michigan in Big Ten title game After Elliott Cadeau's basket just before the halftime buzzer tied the score at 38, Purdue responded with a second-half haymaker out of the gate. More: Why Purdue basketball guard Braden Smith is wearing a 41 jersey vs Michigan The Boilermakers scored seven straight points, ignited by Fletcher Loyer's 3-pointer, to open the half. Braden Smith and Trey Kaufman-Renn went to the pick and roll Michigan had no answer for, resulting in four straight made baskets for Kaufman-Renn. Smith's conventional three-point play with 12:06 to go put the Boilermakers ahead 58-46. How many assists does Braden Smith need to break NCAA record? Braden Smith entered Sunday's game against Michigan needing 12 assists to tie Bobby Hurley's NCAA record of 1,076. Smith finished with 11 assists and is now one shy of Hurley's mark. 3 Purdue basketball standouts vs. Michigan in Big Ten title game Trey Kaufman-Renn: Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year Aday Mara met his match in Purdue's fifth-year post. Kaufman-Renn had 20 points on 10-of-15 shooting. Braden Smith: Smith was 0-for-3 before changing out of his ripped No. 3 jersey in exchange for 41. In a new number, Smith went 6 for 9 and scored 14 points to complete a double-double. Oscar Cluff: Purdue's center led the way to Purdue getting to the Big Ten championship game. He capped his impressive week with a 21-point, five-rebound performance. Sam King covers sports for the Journal & Co
